coredata.dc:description: This study introduces a nanocarrier system (G/PEG/Cu.CQDs@CUR) designed to enhance curcumin (CUR) delivery for glioblastoma treatment. The nanocarrier, synthesized via the W/O/W (Water-in-Oil-in-Water) double emulsion technique, incorporates gelatin (G), polyethylene glycol (PEG), and copper-doped carbon quantum dots (Cu.CQDs) to improve biocompatibility, stability, and pH-dependent medication release. Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) confirmed an average size of 165 nm with a polydispersity index (PDI) of 0.36, while zeta potential analysis indicated a surface charge of −50 mV, ensuring colloidal stability. Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy validated the presence of functional groups, and X-ray diffraction (XRD) confirmed the crystalline structure of Cu.CQDs. Kinetic release studies demonstrated pH-dependent behavior, with drug release reaching 97 % at pH 5.4 and 67 % at a pH of 7.4 over 96 h. The Baker-Lonsdale model produced the greather match for pH 7.4 (R2 = 0.9967), indicating matrix-controlled release, while the Korsmeyer-Peppas model was the best fit at pH 5.4 (R2 = 0.9786), suggesting diffusion-controlled release. field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) imaging confirmed spherical, aggregated particles with porous surfaces, indicating potential for controlled drug release. MTT assays showed over 90 % viability in L929 fibroblasts, while U87-MG glioblastoma cells exhibited reduced viability (55 %) following treatment with G/PEG/Cu.CQDs@CUR. These findings suggest that the G/PEG/Cu.CQDs@CUR nanocarrier is a promising candidate for targeted and sustained drug delivery in brain cancer therapy.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FTIR spectra of (A) Cu.CQDs, (B) G, (C) G/PEG composite, (D) G/PEG/Cu.CQDs, and (E) G/PEG/Cu.CQDs@CUR.
XRD patterns of (A) Cu.CQDs, (B) G, (C) G/PEG composite, (D) G/PEG/Cu.CQDs, and (E) G/PEG/Cu.CQDs@CUR.
FESEM images of synthesized nanocarriers at different magnifications. (A) High magnification (100.0 kx, scale bar 200 nm). (B) Lower magnification (50.0 kx, scale bar 500 nm).
DLS size distribution (A) and zeta potential (B) of the synthesized nanocarriers.

Cumulative release profile of the encapsulated drug from G/PEG/Cu.CQDs nanocarriers at pH 7.4 and pH 5.4 over 100 h. The graph highlights the pH-dependent release behavior, indicating enhanced drug release at acidic (pH 5.4) as opposed to neutral (pH 7.4) circumstances. Error bars represent standard deviations from triplicate measurements.

The drug release data were fitted to various kinetic models, with the green and red lines representing neutral and acidic pH conditions, respectively. The results are expressed as mean ± SD based on three independent measurements.

Cell viability of U87-MG and L929 cells following treatment with different formulations, including control, G, G/PEG, G/PEG/Cu.CQDs, G/PEG/Cu.CQDs@Cur, and free curcumin (Cur free). Results are expressed as percentages, with error bars representing standard deviations from triplicate experiments. (*** imply p-value < 0.0001 compared to the control group, also $$ represent p-value < 0.0001 compared to G/PEG/Cu.CQDs@Cur) (Mean values ± SD, n = 3).
Synthesis of drug-loaded G/PEG/Cu.CQDs@CUR nanoparticles using the W/O/W emulsion method.

Summary of mathematical models used to analyze drug release kinetics, including their equations, descriptions, and key parameters. These models provide insights into the mechanisms governing the release profiles of the studied nanocarriers.

row.entry: Characterizes optimal release with a constant rate over time.
Mt: Cumulative drug released at time t; M0: Initial drug amount; K0: Zero-order constant
Release rate varies with concentration, decreasing over time.
Mt: Cumulative drug released at time t; M0: Initial drug amount; K1: First-order release constant
Relates cumulative release to square root of time, suitable for matrix systems with minimal matrix dissolution.
M∞: Release at infinite time; Mt: Release at time t; KH: Higuchi release constant
Models’ dissolution-dominated systems where surface area diminishes while geometry remains constant.
Mi: Initial drug quantity; Mr: Drug remaining at time t; KHC: Hixson–Crowell release constant
Semi-empirical model describing drug release exponentially, adaptable to unknown mechanisms based on exponent n: Diffusion if n ≤ 0.5; Swelling and diffusion for 0.5 < n < 1; Swelling-only if n = 1.
Mt: Drug release at t; M∞: Release at infinite time; K: Korsmeyer–Peppas constant; nnn: Release exponent
Modified Higuchi model for spherical matrices like microspheres.
Mt: Release at time t; M∞: Release at infinite time; KBL: Baker–Lonsdale constant
Predicts release in finite time with shape modifications: Exponential (β = 1), Sigmoidal (β > 1), or Parabolic (β < 1).
Mt: Release at t; M∞: Release at infinite time; α: Timescale; β: Curve shape indicator

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study introduces a nanocarrier system (G/PEG/Cu.CQDs@CUR) designed to enhance curcumin (CUR) delivery for glioblastoma treatment. The nanocarrier, synthesized via the W/O/W (Water-in-Oil-in-Water) double emulsion technique, incorporates gelatin (G), polyethylene glycol (PEG), and copper-doped carbon quantum dots (Cu.CQDs) to improve biocompatibility, stability, and pH-dependent medication release. Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) confirmed an average size of 165 nm with a polydispersity index (PDI) of 0.36, while zeta potential analysis indicated a surface charge of −50 mV, ensuring colloidal stability. Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy validated the presence of functional groups, and X-ray diffraction (XRD) confirmed the crystalline structure of Cu.CQDs. Kinetic release studies demonstrated pH-dependent behavior, with drug release reaching 97 % at pH 5.4 and 67 % at a pH of 7.4 over 96 h. The Baker-Lonsdale model produced the greather match for pH 7.4 (R2 = 0.9967), indicating matrix-controlled release, while the Korsmeyer-Peppas model was the best fit at pH 5.4 (R2 = 0.9786), suggesting diffusion-controlled release. field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) imaging confirmed spherical, aggregated particles with porous surfaces, indicating potential for controlled drug release. MTT assays showed over 90 % viability in L929 fibroblasts, while U87-MG glioblastoma cells exhibited reduced viability (55 %) following treatment with G/PEG/Cu.CQDs@CUR. These findings suggest that the G/PEG/Cu.CQDs@CUR nanocarrier is a promising candidate for targeted and sustained drug delivery in brain cancer therapy.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer remains one of the leading causes of mortality worldwide, with brain tumors representing one of the most challenging forms due to their aggressive progression, diffuse infiltration, and limited therapeutic options. Glioblastoma, the most prevalent and malignant form of brain tumor, is characterized by rapid proliferation, extensive invasiveness, and pronounced resistance to conventional therapies such as chemotherapy, radiotherapy, and surgical resection [1]. The effectiveness of these standard treatments is further constrained by the presence of the blood–brain barrier (BBB), a highly selective physiological membrane that severely limits the penetration of therapeutic agents into the brain parenchyma [2]. In addition, the complex tumor microenvironment—which promotes cellular heterogeneity, immune suppression, and hypoxia—presents further obstacles to successful therapeutic intervention [3]. As a result, glioblastoma is associated with poor prognosis, frequent tumor recurrence, and high systemic toxicity, emphasizing the urgent need for novel, targeted, and effective drug delivery strategies [4].

Recent advances in nanomedicine have introduced nanocarrier-based platforms as innovative approaches to overcome these therapeutic limitations. Nanocarriers are capable of improving drug solubility, extending systemic circulation, and enhancing the permeability of therapeutic agents across physiological barriers such as the BBB [5]. Moreover, they enable controlled, site-specific drug release while minimizing systemic side effects, primarily through passive targeting mechanisms like the enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect or active targeting via receptor-ligand interactions [4]. Among various delivery systems, polymer-based and functional nanomaterial-integrated nanocarriers have gained significant attention due to their tunable properties, biocompatibility, and capacity for sustained and stimuli-responsive drug release [5].

Despite this progress, multiple challenges remain in optimizing nanocarriers for brain tumor treatment. Many reported systems demonstrate limitations such as insufficient drug loading, premature release in circulation, instability in biological fluids, and lack of tumor specificity. Various strategies have been proposed to address these shortcomings, including the design of lipid-based nanoparticles, polymeric micelles, dendrimer complexes, and quantum dot-based carriers. For example, transferrin-conjugated nanocarriers have been explored to facilitate receptor-mediated transcytosis across the BBB and increase tumor localization [6], while intranasal administration of self-assembled nano-coacervates has been suggested as a non-invasive route to bypass the BBB entirely [7]. Although these approaches show promise, very few successfully integrate biocompatibility, high drug retention, physiological stability, selective cytotoxicity, and controlled release into a single system.

In response to these unmet needs, the present study introduces a composite nanocarrier system, G/PEG/Cu.CQDs@CUR, specifically engineered to enhance the delivery of curcumin (CUR) in glioblastoma therapy. This system incorporates gelatin (G), polyethylene glycol (PEG), copper-doped carbon quantum dots (Cu.CQDs), and curcumin, each selected for their individual functionalities and synergistic contribution to the overall therapeutic efficacy. G, a biodegradable protein derived from collagen, is widely utilized in drug delivery due to its ability to form hydrogels, entrap bioactive molecules, and degrade in response to environmental stimuli [8]. It also provides chemical flexibility for further functionalization, while maintaining low immunogenicity and excellent biocompatibility [9,10]. PEG is employed as a steric stabilizer and solubilizing agent, forming a hydrophilic corona around nanoparticles to reduce protein adsorption, prevent immune clearance, and extend systemic circulation [11,12]. Its water solubility also facilitates uniform dispersion and suitability for intravenous administration [13,14].

Cu.CQDs are incorporated as multifunctional nanocomponents to enhance both therapeutic and diagnostic performance. As ultra-small carbonaceous particles bearing abundant functional groups, CQDs exhibit strong photoluminescence, high surface area, and the capacity to cross the BBB due to their nanoscale dimensions and surface charge [15]. Copper doping of CQDs further enhances their biological activity, as copper ions catalyze the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS), promoting oxidative stress, mitochondrial dysfunction, and apoptotic cell death in tumor tissues [16]. This mechanism complements the pharmacological profile of curcumin, enabling synergistic anti-tumor action. Additionally, Cu.CQDs can participate in photothermal and photodynamic therapy modalities, providing multimodal therapeutic advantages in glioblastoma management [17].

CUR is a natural hydrophobic polyphenol derived from the rhizome of Curcuma longa (turmeric), with well-documented anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and anticancer properties [18]. However, its clinical translation is limited by extremely poor aqueous solubility, rapid systemic metabolism, and low bioavailability [19]. Encapsulating CUR into a structured nanocarrier matrix significantly enhances its physicochemical stability, prolongs its half-life, and facilitates its accumulation in tumor tissues. In the G/PEG/Cu.CQDs nanocarrier developed here, CUR is effectively embedded within a stabilized polymeric network that protects it from enzymatic degradation and promotes pH-responsive release in acidic tumor environments, thereby increasing its therapeutic efficacy while minimizing side effects [20].

The nanocarrier was synthesized using a double emulsion (W/O/W) solvent evaporation technique, which enables the co-encapsulation of both hydrophilic and hydrophobic drugs [21]. This method offers several advantages including high encapsulation efficiency, tunable particle size, and preservation of bioactive compound functionality [22]. The resulting nanoparticles displayed colloidal stability and uniform morphology, ensuring their suitability for intravenous administration. Furthermore, the system demonstrated pH-sensitive behavior, allowing CUR to be selectively released under acidic conditions typically observed in the tumor microenvironment, while remaining stable at physiological pH [23]. This characteristic is especially advantageous in glioblastoma treatment, where extracellular acidosis, a hallmark of the tumor niche, can be exploited for site-specific drug activation [24].

In summary, the G/PEG/Cu.CQDs@CUR nanocarrier system offers a rationally engineered solution to several persistent limitations of conventional drug delivery in glioblastoma. It combines biocompatibility, long circulation time, pH-sensitive targeted release, structural integrity, and therapeutic synergy between CUR and Cu.CQDs. The integrated design addresses critical challenges such as low bioavailability, limited BBB permeability, and lack of selectivity. As such, this platform represents a promising avenue for further preclinical investigation and clinical translation in brain cancer therapy [25,26].
All chemicals were purchased from Aldrich, Merck, Gibco, and Solarbio and used with no further purification. Also, U-87MG and normal Astrocyte cells were acquired from the Pasteur Institute, Iran.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Drug-Loaded nanocarriers using the double emulsion technique
ce:section.ce:para: To begin, 0.6 g of G was dissolved in 30 mL of 2 % (v/v) acetic acid under magnetic stirring at 25 °C and 200 rpm for 35 min to obtain a homogeneous 2 % (w/v) G solution. Then, 0.3 g of powdered PEG was added to the gelatin solution and mixed until fully dissolved. The resulting solution was subjected to ultrasonic agitation using a 50 W ultrasonic bath at 26 °C for 15 min to ensure uniform dispersion.

Subsequently, 30 mg of previously synthesized Cu.CQDs (as reported and validated in our previous publication [15,27]) was added and stirred until completely homogenized. CUR was then introduced to reach a final drug concentration of 5 µg/mL. The mixture was stirred again for 30 min and sonicated under the same conditions (50 W, 26 °C) for an additional 15 min to yield a uniform Cu.CQDs/PEG/G hydrogel containing CUR.

In the first emulsification step, 10 mL of this prepared hydrogel was added dropwise into 30 mL of olive oil containing 2 % (v/v) SPAN 80 surfactant under vigorous stirring at 200 rpm. This process resulted in the formation of a stable water-in-oil (W/O) emulsion. After 10 min of emulsification, 30 mL of distilled water (as the external aqueous phase) was gradually added dropwise while stirring continued to create the W/O/W double emulsion. The mixture was then allowed to stand undisturbed for 20 min to promote phase separation.

The oil phase was carefully removed using a pipette, and the nanocarriers were isolated from the aqueous phase by centrifugation at 6000 rpm for 10 min. To obtain a dry powder form, the collected nanocarriers were frozen at − 20 °C and then subjected to freeze-drying using a laboratory lyophilizer.

These synthesis conditions including surfactant concentration, stirring speed, temperature, and sonication time were optimized to ensure reproducibility and preserve the structural and functional integrity of the drug-loaded nanocarriers. The complete fabrication process is illustrated schematically in Scheme 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluating the drug encapsulation and loading efficiency
ce:section.ce:para:
To evaluate the efficiency of encapsulation (EE%) and loading efficiency (LE%) of the CUR-loaded nanocarriers, 1 mg of the prepared nanocarriers was dispersed in 1 mL of phosphate-buffered saline (PBS). Subsequently, 1 mL of ethyl acetate was included in the solution to dissolve any unencapsulated CUR. The OD, or optical density, of the resultant mixture was determined using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer at a wavelength of 419 nm. The concentration of CUR in the solution was determined by referencing a previously established calibration curve for CUR. Encapsulation efficiency and loading efficiency values were determined using Equations (1) and (2), respectively [27].

In this context, wt, CUR indicates the total quantity of CUR, wun, CUR epresents the amount of unencapsulated CUR, and wt, NC corresponds to the total weight of the CUR-loaded nanocarriers.

To study the release kinetics of CUR over time, nanocarriers loaded with CUR were first dispersed in an aqueous medium. A 5 mL aliquot of the mixture was cautiously put into a dialysis bag with a 12 kDa molecular weight cut-off (MWCO). After that, the dialysis bag was submerged in a tube that held 30 mL of a buffer solution, prepared by mixing phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) and ethanol in a 4:1 vol ratio. Release studies were performed at different pH levels (5.4, 7.4, and 8.5), with the buffer pH adjusted using citric acid and sodium hydroxide solutions.

The tubes were cultured at 37 °C while gently agitated to maintain uniform conditions. Samples of 300 µL were withdrawn from the buffer solution throughout particular periods of time (0.5, 1, 2, 3, 6, 12, 24, 48, 72, and 96 h) to assess the amount of CUR released. Each sample was analyzed by measuring its absorbance at 419 nm with a UV–Vis a spectrum analyzer. To ensure that the total volume of the buffer remained constant, an equivalent volume (300 µL) of fresh buffer was added back to the tube after each sampling. The CUR concentrations in the samples were analyzed based on a standard calibration curve. Finally, the percentage of CUR released was determined by comparing the cumulative CUR amount in the release medium to the initially loaded CUR, as described in Equation (3)[28].

Here, Cr represents the amount of CUR released, while ​ Cl denotes the amount of CUR initially loaded into the nanocarriers.

The experimental release data were analyzed by fitting them to a range of mathematical models. This included the application of several empirical and semi-empirical models, such as Higuchi, Weibull, Korsmeyer-Peppas, Baker-Lonsdale, First-order, Hixson-Crowell, and Zero-order models. The details of each model, including their equations, descriptions, and key parameters, are provided in Table. 1 The selection of the most suitable model was guided by evaluating the R2 values, ensuring that the chosen model accurately represented the observed release patterns.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell culture and cytotoxicity assessment in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: The cytotoxic effects of CUR-containing nanocarriers were evaluated using the MTT assay. This assay relies on the enzymatic reduction of the MTT reagent into Forman crystals that are insoluble due to mitochondrial dehydrogenases in living cells. The extent of formazan formation correlates with cell viability, where higher formazan production reflects lower cytotoxicity and greater cell survival.

This study employed two cell lines: human glioblastoma (U87-MG) and murine fibroblast (L929). Both cell lines were cultivated in Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM), which was enhanced with 1 % penicillin–streptomycin solution and 10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS). After the cells reached confluence, they were centrifuged for 5 min at 1500 rpm, underwent treatment with trypsin-EDTA over 5 min to separate the cells, and then rinsed with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS). To promote adhesion, the collected cells were planted at a density of 5000 cells in each well onto plates with 96 wells and cultured for 24 h.

Following the initial incubation, the culture media were replaced with 100 µL of test samples. The tested solutions included free CUR, G solution, G/PEG blended solution, G/PEG/ Cu.CQDs nanocomposite, and CUR-containing G/PEG/Cu.CQDs nanocarriers. The negative control consisted of wells that contained solely cultural media. The test samples had been incubated with the cells for all three of the days. Following the removal of the medium at each time point, 100 µL of the MTT procedure was applied to each well. The sample containing MTT was carefully withdrawn after the formazan crystals had formed after four hours, and 100 µL of dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) was added to help disintegrate the crystals. After that, the plates were incubated for 15 min at 25 °C in the dark.

The OD was evaluated at 570 nm using a spectrum analyzer. Cell viability was calculated relative to the control group using Equation (4)[36].
(4)
Viability
( % )

A s

In this context, As represents the absorbance of the sample, while Ac denotes the absorbance of the control.

Field emission scanning electron microscopy was used to analyze the structural characteristics of the CUR-loaded nanocarriers (FESEM, Mira3, Tescan, Czech Republic). To improve conductivity, a small coating of gold was applied to the nanocarrier powder before imaging. Images were taken with an accelerating voltage of 20 kV at different magnifications.

Using Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Perkin Elmer, USA), the chemical reactions between the polymers and CUR inside the nanocarrier matrix were examined. Spectra were captured at a resolution of 1 cm−1 across a range of 500–4000 cm−1.

The crystalline structure of the nanocarriers was analyzed using X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8 Advance, Germany). XRD patterns were acquired within a 2θ range of 5°–80° at a scanning rate of 0.02°/s, utilizing CuKα radiation with a wavelength of 1.5406 Å.

The SZ-100z analyzer (Horiba, Japan) was used to detect the zeta potential and dynamic light scattering (DLS) in order to ascertain the surface charge, polydispersity index (PDI), and the particle size of the nanocarriers. To guarantee adequate suspension, the nanocarrier powder was ultrasonically disseminated in an aqueous media prior to measurement. A temperature of 25 °C was used for all studies.

All measurements were conducted in triplicate to ensure statistical reliability and reproducibility. The resulting data are expressed as mean values ± standard deviation (SD), reflecting the consistency and variability within the experimental replicates.

Fig. 1 presents the FTIR spectra of Cu.CQDs (A), G (B), G/PEG (C), G/PEG/Cu.CQDs (D), and G/PEG/Cu.CQDs@CUR (E). The analysis of these spectra provides insights into the chemical interactions, functional groups, and successful incorporation of CUR within the nanocarrier system. The FTIR spectrum of Cu.CQDs (A) reveals prominent absorption bands around 3420 cm−1, Associated with stretching vibrations of O–H and N–H, signifying the existence of amine and hydroxyl groups [37]. The peaks at 1390 cm−1 and 1625 cm−1 are due to C=O stretching and C–N bending, confirming the presence of carbonyl and amide groups. The absorption near 1100 cm−1 suggests C–O stretching, commonly observed in carbon-based materials [38]. Additionally, small peaks observed in the region below 1000 cm−1 may correspond to Cu–O vibrations. Peaks around 600–800 cm−1 are indicative of potential Cu–C or Cu–N bonds, suggesting the integration of copper within the carbon or nitrogen matrix [39]. These low-wavenumber absorptions provide evidence for the presence of copper in the form of copper oxide or copper-carbon bonding within the Cu.CQDs structure. The FTIR spectrum of G (B) displays characteristic peaks at 3280 cm−1, corresponding to N–H stretching vibrations, with a peak at 1640 cm−1 signifying amide I (C=O stretching). Amide II (N–H bending) and amide III (C–N stretching) vibrations appear at 1550 cm−1 and 1240 cm−1, respectively. The peak near 2925 cm−1 represents C–H stretching [40]. In the G/PEG spectrum(C), the characteristic peaks of gelatin are retained, with additional broadening around 1100 cm−1, ascribed to the PEG backbone's C–O–C stretching [41]. A minor shift in the N–H stretching band near 3280 cm−1 suggests hydrogen bonding between G and PEG molecules. The incorporation of Cu.CQDs into the G/PEG matrix(D) results in noticeable shifts in the FTIR spectrum. The O–H and N–H stretching bands around 3420 cm−1 become broader and more intense, indicating strong hydrogen bonding between the Cu.CQDs and the G/PEG network. A little change in the amide I peak from 1640 cm−1 to 1620 cm−1 indicates that the carbonyl groups of G and Cu are interacting. The CQDs. The enhancement of the C–N stretching peak around 1240 cm−1 further confirms the successful integration of Cu.CQDs. The FTIR spectrum of the final formulation(E), G/PEG/Cu.CQDs@CUR, shows distinct peaks that confirm the encapsulation of CUR. O-H stretching is shown by the prominent peak at 3500 cm−1, indicative of phenolic groups in CUR [42]. The peak at 1625 cm−1 (C=O stretching) overlaps with the amide I band, confirming the interaction between CUR and the carrier matrix [43]. Additionally, Those responsible for the peaks at 1508 cm−1 and 1270 cm−1 include C=C aromatic stretching and C–O–C bending, respectively, characteristic of CUR’s structure [44]. These peaks, along with the preservation of bands from the G/PEG/Cu.CQDs composite, validate the successful encapsulation of CUR within the nanocarrier.

Fig. 2 displays the XRD patterns of Cu.CQDs (A), G (B), G/PEG (C), G/PEG/Cu.CQDs (D), and G/PEG/Cu.CQDs@CUR (E). This analysis provides essential insights into the crystalline and amorphous characteristics of the nanocarrier components and their structural transformations following CUR loading.

The XRD pattern of Cu.CQDs (A) reveals sharp diffraction peaks at 26.5°, 32°, 45.5°, 56°, and 75° (2θ), indicative of their highly crystalline nature. The peak at 26.5° corresponds to graphitic carbon's (002) reflection, confirming the presence of sp2-hybridized carbon [45]. The peaks at 32° and 45.5° suggest copper oxide (CuO) phases, while the reflections at 56° and 75° indicate additional copper oxide or metallic copper phases [46]. The diffraction pattern of G (B) exhibits a broad peak at 20° (2θ), characteristic of an amorphous structure. The lack of sharp reflections suggests disordered polymer chains, which is typical of biopolymers [47]. In the G/PEG composite (C), two broad peaks are observed at 20° and 23°. The retention of the amorphous peak at 20° indicates that the G structure remains largely disordered after blending with PEG. The additional peak at 23° corresponds to the crystalline region of PEG, suggesting partial crystallization within the composite [48]. The XRD pattern of the G/PEG/Cu.CQDs composite (D) shows peaks at 20° and 23°, without new crystalline reflections from Cu.CQDs. The absence of Cu.CQDs specific peaks indicates their effective dispersion within the G/PEG matrix, preventing aggregation and maintaining the overall amorphous structure. Following CUR encapsulation, the XRD pattern of G/PEG/Cu.CQDs@CUR (E) retains the broad peaks at 20° and 23°, similar to the previous composite. The absence of sharp crystalline peaks from CUR indicates that it is molecularly dispersed within the polymeric matrix in an amorphous state [49]. This distribution prevents CUR crystallization, enhancing its solubility and bioavailability. The reduced intensity of the PEG associated peak at 23° suggests that CUR may interfere with PEG crystallization, further promoting the amorphous nature of the final nanocarrier [50].

FESEM analysis, as shown in Fig. 3 , reveals the morphological characteristics of the synthesized nanocarriers, confirming the successful formation of spherical and aggregated particles. The images show particles with varying surface textures, indicative of W/O/W emulsion technique utilized in the fabrication process. At lower magnification, the particles exhibit a rough, porous surface, suggesting the presence of voids or channels that could facilitate controlled drug release. Higher magnification highlights the formation of smaller aggregated nanoparticles, which may reflect incomplete separation during synthesis or secondary emulsification effects.

The observed porosity and surface roughness support the potential for sustained drug release, while the absence of excessive aggregation suggests effective surfactant action during emulsification [51]. These results confirm the structural integrity of the nanocarriers, aligning with the desired characteristics for drug delivery applications.

Fig. 4 illustrates the DLS size distribution (A) and zeta potential (B) of the synthesized nanocarriers, providing essential information on their stability, homogeneity, and surface charge characteristics.

The DLS results ( Fig. 4 . A) display a sharp, monomodal peak at 165 nm, indicating the average hydrodynamic diameter of the nanocarriers. The polydispersity index (PDI) is measured at 0.36, reflecting moderate size distribution. This value suggests a relatively uniform particle population with minimal aggregation, which aligns with the desired characteristics for drug delivery systems. The single, narrow peak signifies the effectiveness of the W/O/W emulsion method in producing consistently sized nanocarriers. A PDI below 0.4 indicates an acceptable level of uniformity, confirming that the fabrication process yielded particles with predictable and reproducible sizes [52].

The zeta potential measurement (Fig. 4 . B) shows a distinct peak at approximately −50 mV, indicating a highly negative surface charge. This value signifies excellent colloidal stability, as particles with zeta potentials greater than ± 30 mV typically experience strong electrostatic repulsion, preventing aggregation [53]. The observed negative surface charge is primarily attributed to the presence of ionizable and polar functional groups distributed across the nanocarrier surface. G contains carboxyl (–COOH) and amino (–NH2) groups; under neutral to slightly basic pH conditions, the carboxyl groups deprotonate to form negatively charged –COO− moieties, which significantly contribute to surface electronegativity [10]. PEG, while being neutral, improves colloidal stabilization by forming a hydrophilic corona that sterically hinders particle aggregation [14]. In addition, Cu.CQDs possess oxygen-containing groups such as hydroxyl (–OH) and carboxyl functionalities on their surface, which also deprotonate and enhance the negative charge. The combined effect of electrostatic repulsion (from deprotonated acidic groups) and steric stabilization (from PEG chains) accounts for the high zeta potential and excellent colloidal stability of the nanocarriers in aqueous systems [15]. While this measurement suggests good short-term stability, it is equally critical to consider how this colloidal behavior is preserved over time, particularly in biologically relevant media. In this regard, the nanocarriers were observed to remain visually stable and free of aggregation for at least 72 h when incubated in PBS) pH 7.4) and in DMEM supplemented with 10 % FBS, simulating physiological conditions. The absence of visible sedimentation or turbidity under these conditions indicates that the negative surface charge remains effective in maintaining interparticle repulsion, even in the presence of proteins and electrolytes. This stability is essential for ensuring consistent performance during in vitro or in vivo applications, where loss of colloidal integrity can compromise biodistribution, bioavailability, and therapeutic efficacy.

Furthermore, beyond ensuring colloidal stability, the highly negative surface charge of Cu.CQDs plays a crucial role in governing the supramolecular organization of the nanocarriers. Through a delicate balance of electrostatic repulsion and attractive interfacial forces with G and PEG, the system undergoes controlled self-assembly into stable macromolecular structures. This behavior is driven by minimization of interfacial free energy and dynamic interactions within the polymeric network, resulting in particle sizes larger than the individual CQDs. Importantly, such assembly behavior can be modulated by adjusting formulation parameters including the Cu.CQDs-to-polymer ratio, ionic strength, and environmental pH offering tunability of the final particle size for application-specific optimization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of Cu.CQDs Nanomaterials on CUR encapsulation efficiency and drug loading capacity
ce:section.ce:para: The data presented in Table. 2 highlight the significant impact of incorporating Cu.CQDs into the G and PEG matrix on both loading and encapsulation efficiency of CUR. In the absence of Cu.CQDs, the drug loading capacity of the G/PEG/CUR nanocarrier was measured at 37.50 %, while the inclusion of Cu.CQDs in the G/PEG/Cu.CQDs@CUR formulation increased the loading capacity to 46.00 %, reflecting an enhancement of + 8.50 %. Similarly, the encapsulation efficiency showed a substantial improvement, rising from 71.00 % in the G/PEG/CUR system to 85.25 % in the Cu.CQDs-containing formulation, corresponding to a + 14.25 % increase.

This marked improvement can be attributed to the physicochemical properties of Cu.CQDs. As a multifunctional nanomaterial, Cu.CQDs possess a high surface area and abundant active sites, which enhance the interaction between CUR molecules and the nanocarrier matrix [54]. The interactions between the aromatic structure of CUR through π-π stacking and hydrogen bonding and CQDs component of Cu.CQDs likely facilitate the encapsulation process, providing stronger retention of CUR molecules within the matrix. Furthermore, the copper ions in Cu.CQDs may contribute to these effects through electrostatic interactions, enhancing the stability of the encapsulated drug.

The observed increase in drug loading efficiency is also consistent with the role of Cu.CQDs as a stabilizing agent. Their ability to improve the compatibility between the hydrophobic CUR molecules and the hydrophilic G/PEG matrix likely reduces drug loss during the formulation process. Moreover, the inherent fluorescent properties of Cu.CQDs may improve the distribution of CUR within the matrix, ensuring a more uniform encapsulation [55].

Additionally, the structural integrity of the G/PEG/Cu.CQDs nanocarrier may also play a crucial role. G provides a biocompatible matrix, effectively encapsulating active compounds [56], while PEG enhances the hydrophilicity and stability of the carrier [57]. The incorporation of Cu.CQDs further enhances these properties by improving the matrix's drug-binding capacity and its ability to sustain high encapsulation efficiency.

The enhanced encapsulation efficiency and loading capacity observed in the Cu.CQDs containing system not only reflect the beneficial interactions between CUR and the nanocarrier but also suggest a potential improvement in the therapeutic performance of the formulation. The higher encapsulation efficiency implies a more controlled and sustained release of CUR, which is essential for maximizing its therapeutic effect while minimizing side effects [58].

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro Evaluation of CUR release Dynamics from nanocarriers
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5 illustrates the release profiles of CUR from nanocarriers at pH 5.4 and 7.4 over 96 h. The release behavior demonstrates significant pH sensitivity, critical for targeted drug delivery.

At pH 5.4, CUR release exhibited a rapid initial phase, with 46 % of the drug released during the initial 6 h. The cause of this explosive explosion is the higher degradation rate of the nanocarrier matrix in acidic conditions, likely facilitated by the disruption of hydrogen bonds and interactions within the polymeric structure [59]. The acidic pH may accelerate the disassembly of the nanocarrier matrix, enhancing the diffusion of CUR molecules from the surface and near-surface regions [60]. Following this initial phase, the release transitioned to a more controlled, sustained release pattern. By the 24-hour mark, 76 % of the CUR had been released, reaching 93 % at 72 h and nearing complete release (97 %) at 96 h. This two-phase release behavior—a rapid initial burst followed by prolonged release—provides an optimal therapeutic profile for cancer treatment. The initial burst delivers a high concentration of CUR to the tumor site, facilitating rapid cancer cell apoptosis, while the sustained release maintains therapeutic levels over an extended period, reducing the likelihood of tumor recurrence [61]. In contrast, CUR release at pH 7.4 was significantly slower, demonstrating the stability of the nanocarrier system under neutral conditions. The release was limited to 16 % in the first 6 h and increased gradually to 37 % at 24 h, 58 % at 72 h, and 67 % by 96 h. The slower release rate at neutral pH is a direct result of the structural integrity of the nanocarrier matrix, which remains stable in physiological conditions, minimizing premature drug release. This controlled release behavior at pH 7.4 ensures that the nanocarrier retains CUR during circulation, reducing systemic side effects and improving drug delivery specificity [62].

The stark difference in release profiles between pH 5.4 and pH 7.4 highlights the pH-responsive nature of the nanocarrier system. The accelerated release under acidic conditions is advantageous for targeting the tumor microenvironment, which typically exhibits a lower pH due to hypoxia and anaerobic glycolysis [63]. This responsiveness ensures that CUR is preferentially released at the tumor site, while the slower release at neutral pH minimizes off-target effects in healthy tissues [64].

The initial burst release at pH 5.4 is likely due to CUR positioned close to the surface of the nanocarriers, which are less tightly bound and readily diffuse into the external medium. As the release progresses, CUR molecules from deeper within the nanocarrier matrix are released more gradually, driven by matrix degradation and diffusion processes. At pH 7.4, the lower degradation rate of the matrix results in a more uniform and prolonged release, reflecting the stability of the system under physiological conditions [65].

The observed pH-responsive behavior can be attributed to the synergistic roles of the nanocarrier components. G provides a biodegradable and biocompatible matrix, facilitating the encapsulation of CUR and ensuring controlled release. PEG enhances the hydrophilicity and stability of the nanocarrier, preventing premature degradation in neutral environments. The incorporation of Cu.CQDs further enhances the system's pH sensitivity. The high surface area and functional groups of Cu.CQDs promote interactions with CUR and the polymer matrix, facilitating faster degradation and release in acidic conditions while maintaining stability at neutral pH. Additionally, the presence of copper ions may contribute to the destabilization of the matrix at lower pH, further enhancing the release of CUR [66]. Based on the release profiles and material characteristics, the predominant mechanism of CUR release under acidic conditions appears to be a combination of matrix erosion and diffusion. At pH 5.4, the accelerated release suggests that acid-induced degradation of the G-based matrix compromises the structural integrity of the nanocarriers, resulting in matrix erosion that facilitates CUR escape. This degradation is likely initiated by the protonation of carboxyl groups and disruption of intermolecular hydrogen bonding, leading to fragmentation of the polymer network. Simultaneously, diffusion plays a significant role, especially in the initial burst phase, where CUR located near the surface rapidly migrates into the medium [19]. At neutral pH (7.4), the matrix remains structurally intact due to reduced hydrolysis and enhanced PEG-mediated stabilization, resulting in a slower, diffusion-dominated release profile [12]. Therefore, the observed pH-responsive behavior is governed by an erosion–diffusion mechanism, modulated by environmental pH and the physicochemical interactions within the nanocarrier matrix.

The formation of the nanocarrier relies on the cooperative assembly of its components through multiple non-covalent interactions. G, rich in carboxyl and amino groups, interacts with PEG via hydrogen bonding and chain entanglement, forming a semi-interpenetrated network. PEG contributes to the hydrophilicity and steric stabilization of the outer shell, limiting premature degradation in neutral conditions [9]. Cu.CQDs, containing abundant surface hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, are embedded within the G matrix and interact both with CUR and the polymer chains via hydrogen bonding and van der Waals forces. CUR itself is entrapped through hydrophobic interactions and π–π stacking with the aromatic residues of G and the carbon core of Cu.CQDs. These synergistic interactions not only enable efficient encapsulation of CUR but also modulate its release in response to pH [18]. The structural integrity at physiological pH is primarily maintained by PEG and gelatin interactions, while under acidic conditions, protonation of G residues and increased mobility of the matrix facilitate controlled degradation and release. This molecular-level architecture underpins the functional behavior observed in the release study. In addition to influencing drug release, the surface charge and functionality of Cu.CQDs play a key role in determining the size and structural organization of the nanocarriers [16]. The negative surface potential arising from carboxyl and hydroxyl groups contributes to electrostatic repulsion, minimizing aggregation and promoting dispersion. However, during the formation of the composite, non-covalent interactions with G and PEG drive a spontaneous self-assembly process that results in macro-sized structures observed in DLS measurements. This size increase is further modulated by polymer concentration, the Cu.CQDs-to-polymer ratio, and environmental pH, which collectively affect the compactness and swelling behavior of the matrix. Thus, the final particle size is not solely a reflection of the nanoparticle core but is governed by a dynamic balance between electrostatic stabilization, polymer network formation, and solvent interactions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Kinetics of CUR Release: An in vitro study
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the most appropriate release model for CUR-loaded nanocarriers, the experimental data were fitted to various kinetic equations. The coefficient of determination (R2) values corresponding to pH 7.4 and pH 5.4 are provided in Table 3 , and Fig. 6 shows the related release profiles.

The Baker-Lonsdale model best describes the release profile at pH 7.4. with an R2value of 0.9967, closely followed by the Higuchi model (R2 = 0.996). The superior correlation with the Baker-Lonsdale model suggests that the drug release follows a diffusion-controlled mechanism from spherical matrices. This result implies that CUR release under neutral conditions is primarily governed by Fickian diffusion, where the drug diffuses gradually through the stable nanocarrier matrix. Such controlled and sustained release is ideal for minimizing premature drug loss in physiological conditions [34].

With an R2 value of 0.9786, the Korsmeyer-Peppas model best described the release profile of CUR from the nanocarriers at pH 5.4. The model equation may be written as follows:
ln
( f )
= 0.2135 ln
t
+ 0.9813
The slope of the equation, n = 0.2135, is a critical parameter in the Korsmeyer-Peppas model, representing the release exponent. When n is less than 0.5, the drug release mechanism is primarily Fickian diffusion. In this case, the release exponent indicates that CUR release under acidic conditions follows a diffusion-dominated process, driven by the concentration gradient and the destabilization of the nanocarrier in the acidic environment [33].

The high R2 value confirms the strong correlation between the experimental data and the Korsmeyer-Peppas model, further supporting the accuracy of the observed release behavior. Under acidic conditions (pH 5.4), the nanocarrier system undergoes matrix destabilization, likely due to the presence of G and pH-sensitive interactions with Cu.CQDs, resulting in enhanced CUR diffusion. This behavior aligns with the observed accelerated release of CUR at pH 5.4, as reported earlier.

The cytotoxicity of CUR-loaded nanocarriers was assessed using the MTT assay on U87-MG glioblastoma cells and L929 fibroblast cells to evaluate both their anticancer activity and biocompatibility. The results, summarized in Fig. 7 , highlight the distinct effects of various formulations on cell viability.

The control group, along with the G and G/PEG formulations, exhibited 100 % cell viability for both U87-MG and L929 cells, confirming the biocompatibility of the polymeric matrices. This indicates that G and the G/PEG sample do not induce cytotoxic effects, making them safe for use in biomedical applications. The addition of Cu.CQDs in the G/PEG formulation slightly reduced cell viability to 92 % in U87-MG cells and 97 % in L929 cells. This minor decrease can be attributed to the potential oxidative stress induced by Cu.CQDs, although their overall biocompatibility remains high [67,68]. These results confirm the suitability of Cu.CQDs for incorporation into the nanocarrier system.

The viability of U87-MG cells was significantly reduced by the G/PEG/Cu.CQDs@CUR formulation, dropping to 55 %. This demonstrates the strong anticancer activity of the CUR-loaded nanocarriers against glioblastoma cells. In contrast, the L929 cells maintained 94 % viability, reflecting the selective cytotoxicity of the nanocarrier system toward cancer cells while sparing healthy fibroblast cells. This selectivity likely arises from the targeted release of CUR in the acidic tumor microenvironment, facilitated by the pH-responsive behavior of the nanocarriers. Additionally, the preferential uptake of nanocarriers by cancer cells, due to their enhanced metabolic activity, may contribute to this effect.

For the free CUR group, U87-MG cell viability decreased to 62 %, while L929 cells showed a viability of 73 %. Although CUR alone exhibited anticancer activity, its effects were less selective than those of the encapsulated CUR in the G/PEG/Cu.CQDs nanocarriers. The lower viability of L929 cells in the free CUR group highlights the advantages of nanocarrier-based delivery, which improves CUR stability, enhances selectivity, and reduces off-target toxicity.

The observed results can be attributed to the synergistic effects of the nanocarrier components. G provides a biocompatible and biodegradable matrix that ensures effective CUR encapsulation and controlled release [69]. PEG enhances the system's hydrophilicity, stability, and circulation time by reducing premature drug leakage and protecting the nanocarrier from immune clearance [70]. The inclusion of Cu.CQDs introduces pH-responsive behavior, enabling targeted CUR release in acidic tumor microenvironments while maintaining stability in neutral conditions. Additionally, Cu.CQDs may contribute to therapeutic efficacy by enhancing CUR retention in the nanocarrier and generating oxidative stress in cancer cells [71,72]. This combination allows the nanocarrier system to deliver CUR selectively to cancer cells, reducing off-target effects on healthy tissues. The integration of these materials ensures both the biocompatibility of the system and its effectiveness as a targeted anticancer therapy. These findings clearly indicate that the G/PEG/Cu.CQDs@CUR nanocarriers exhibit superior anticancer activity and selectivity compared to free CUR. While free CUR reduced U87-MG cell viability to 62 %, the nanocarrier formulation further decreased it to 55 %. Moreover, the nanocarrier preserved L929 cell viability at 94 %, in contrast to 73 % observed for free CUR. This enhanced performance underscores the advantage of nanoencapsulation in improving therapeutic efficacy while minimizing toxicity to healthy cells.

A rationally designed composite strategy was employed to integrate the distinct yet synergistic properties of G, PEG, and Cu.CQDs into a single nanocarrier platform. G offered a biodegradable and pH-sensitive matrix suited to tumor microenvironments, while PEG enhanced colloidal stability and prolonged systemic circulation. The incorporation of Cu.CQDs introduced pH-responsiveness, improved drug retention, and potential redox-mediated anticancer activity. This synergistic assembly enabled efficient CUR encapsulation, selective release under acidic conditions, and targeted cytotoxicity against glioblastoma cells, representing a clear advantage over conventional nanocarrier systems for brain cancer therapy.

These results demonstrate that the developed nanocarrier system integrates structural integrity, functional responsiveness, and biological selectivity in a single platform. The combination of pH-sensitive release, selective anti-tumor activity, and stable encapsulation of CUR addresses major therapeutic challenges in glioblastoma treatment, including poor drug solubility, low bioavailability, and off-target toxicity. Based on these properties, the system holds strong potential for further translational research and targeted application in brain cancer therapy.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Mahshid Afshari holds an M.Sc. in Organic Chemistry from Amirkabir University of Technology. Her primary research interests focus on nanomedicine and drug delivery systems. She has published research in this field and has worked on the synthesis of novel drug carriers designed for targeted therapeutic applications.

Mehrab pourmadadi is a researcher affiliated with the Protein Research Center at Shahid Beheshti University and Tehran University. His interdisciplinary research focuses on nanobiosensors, drug delivery systems, wound healing materials, and nano-biomaterials. With a strong background in nanobiotechnology and biomedical engineering, he works at the interface of materials science, cancer research, and biotechnology, aiming to develop innovative solutions for diagnostic and therapeutic applications.

Salar Mohammadi Shabestari is a biopolymer researcher specializing in drug delivery systems and tissue engineering. His research focuses on biodegradable polymers, nanocarriers, and bioactive scaffolds designed to enhance controlled drug release and promote tissue regeneration. By integrating advanced polymer chemistry with nanotechnology, he aims to develop next-generation biomaterials for regenerative medicine applications. He holds a Bachelor's degree in Polymer Engineering from Amirkabir University of Technology (Tehran Polytechnic) and a Master's degree in Polymer Engineering from the University of Tehran.

Hamidreza Abdouss is a biopolymer researcher specializing in drug delivery systems and tissue engineering. His research focuses on biodegradable polymers, nanocarriers, and bioactive scaffolds designed to enhance controlled drug release and promote tissue regeneration. By integrating advanced polymer chemistry with nanotechnology, he aims to develop next-generation biomaterials for regenerative medicine applications. He holds a Bachelor's degree in Polymer Engineering from Amirkabir University of Technology (Tehran Polytechnic) and a Master's degree in Polymer Engineering from the University of Tehran.

Majid Abdouss is a full professor in the Department of Chemistry at Amirkabir University of Technology (Tehran Polytechnic). With over 20 years of research experience, his work focuses on polymer chemistry, materials chemistry, and nanotechnology. He has made notable contributions to functional biomaterials, in situ polymerization, nanoparticle engineering, and biopolymer-based drug delivery systems. His research group develops nanostructured materials for drug delivery, biosensing, biomedical devices, and energy applications such as dye-sensitized solar cells.
